courses.title: Master the Essentials
courses.description: Writing, documents, research, and decisions. Five practical skills that make ChatGPT useful for real work, not just demos.

lessons.title: Make ChatGPT Sound Like You
lessons.description: The show-don't-tell prompt engineering pattern: use real examples to capture your voice instead of describing it with adjectives.

sections.content: Stop describing your tone with adjectives. Paste 2–3 things you've actually written and say "match this." ChatGPT pattern-matches from examples — give it examples, not labels.
Show examples, not adjectives. Add anti-patterns ("never say X"). Set up Custom Instructions or a Custom GPT so your voice loads automatically every session.

exercises.prompt: Match each tone descriptor to the sentence that actually sounds that way. Descriptions are abstract -- examples are how ChatGPT learns your voice.
exercises.correct_answer: {"Punchy and opinionated":"Hot take: your standup is a performance review in disguise. Kill it. Ship a Loom instead.","Warm and story-driven":"When I got fired in 2019, I thought my career was over. Turns out it was just starting.","Authoritative and analytical":"The data is clear: remote teams ship 23% faster when async defaults are enforced. Three factors drive this.","Playful and self-deprecating":"I spent six months building the wrong feature. On the bright side, my Jira board has never looked better.","Blunt and contrarian":"Everyone's wrong about onboarding. Your new hire doesn't need a 90-day plan. They need one unambiguous win in week one.","Reflective and personal":"I've been thinking about why I say yes to meetings I don't want. It's not politeness. It's fear dressed up as collaboration."}
exercises.explanation: Every tone lives in the sentence mechanics: punchy writing uses short verbs and hot takes. Warm writing opens with story. Authoritative writing leads with data. Show ChatGPT the sentence patterns, not the adjectives.
hints: The sentence mechanics give it away. Short verbs vs. long clauses. Stat vs. story. Claim vs. question.

exercises.prompt: You're setting up ChatGPT Custom Instructions to capture your writing voice for client emails. Write the custom instructions you'd add.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're setting up ChatGPT Custom Instructions for client emails. You want every email ChatGPT writes for you to match your natural style. Write the custom instructions that define your style rules.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["short","concise","brief","under","words","sentences","length"],"label":"Set length or brevity constraints","points":1},{"keyword_group":["never","don't","avoid","no","stop","skip","without"],"label":"Included anti-patterns to avoid","points":2},{"keyword_group":["value","lead with","open with","start with","first","begin"],"label":"Specified how to open or lead","points":1},{"keyword_group":["tone","voice","casual","formal","direct","peer","friendly","professional"],"label":"Defined the overall tone","points":1},{"keyword_group":["CTA","next step","action","end with","close with","sign off"],"label":"Specified ending or call-to-action style","points":1},{"keyword_group":["example","sample","uploaded","attached","reference","match","like the"],"label":"Referenced the uploaded examples","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Match the tone of the uploaded email examples. Rules: keep emails under 100 words. Lead with value, not pleasantries. Never say 'hope this finds you well' or 'please don't hesitate.' End with a specific next step, not a vague 'let me know.' Tone: direct, peer-to-peer, like texting a professional friend.","tips":["Anti-patterns ('never say X') are often more useful than positive instructions because they cut the most common AI slop.","Include specific example phrases from your own writing to anchor the style.","Specific constraints (word count, opener style, CTA format) remove ambiguity."]}
exercises.explanation: Custom Instructions work best when they combine style rules, anti-patterns, and example phrases from your actual writing. Constraints beat descriptions.
hints: Think about the specific phrases and patterns that make emails sound like AI slop. Tell ChatGPT to avoid those.

exercises.prompt: Walk through the process of getting ChatGPT to match your writing voice, from a generic first attempt to a polished result.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "From AI Slop to Your Voice", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You asked ChatGPT to write a LinkedIn post about hiring. It came back with: 'Hiring is one of the most critical decisions a company can make. The right talent acquisition strategy can accelerate growth, while poor hiring decisions can set a company back significantly.' Classic AI slop. Let's fix it."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The post is technically correct but sounds like it could have come from any AI tool. Zero personality. What's your first move?", "question": "How do you get ChatGPT to rewrite this with personality?", "options": ["Say 'make it better' and hope for the best", "Paste an example of your actual writing and say 'match this tone instead'", "Ask ChatGPT to 'be more creative'", "Add some exclamation points to make it feel energetic"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Showing ChatGPT a real example of your writing gives it a concrete target. 'Make it better' is too vague. 'Be creative' means nothing specific. Exclamation points don't equal personality."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You pasted an example and ChatGPT rewrote it with more personality. Now it reads: 'You don't have a hiring problem. You have a filtering problem. 200 applicants and you're reading every resume like it's a novel.' Better, but it ends with a generic call to action: 'What are your thoughts on hiring? Let me know in the comments!'", "question": "How do you fix the weak ending without losing the strong opening?", "options": ["Rewrite the whole thing from scratch", "Tell ChatGPT: 'The opening is perfect. Keep it. Replace the ending with a specific, opinionated take. No questions begging for engagement.'", "Say 'make the ending better'", "Accept it as good enough"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Protecting good parts while targeting weak parts is the core iteration skill. Telling ChatGPT exactly what to keep and what to change saves you from losing progress."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "Now write the final polish prompt. The post is 90% there but has one sentence that says 'leveraging talent acquisition strategies.' Kill the jargon and make it sound like a real person.", "placeholder": "Write your final polish prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["kill", "remove", "cut", "replace", "drop", "delete", "leveraging", "jargon"], "label": "Targeted the specific jargon", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["plain", "simple", "real", "human", "specific", "concrete", "English"], "label": "Asked for plain language", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["keep", "don't change", "rest is good", "everything else"], "label": "Protected the rest of the post", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Almost perfect. Kill 'leveraging talent acquisition strategies' and replace it with something specific about what you actually do, in plain English. Don't change anything else.", "max_points": 5, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Voice matching is a three-step process: show ChatGPT an example, iterate on specific sections while protecting good parts, then polish individual words. Each round gets more precise.
hints: Think about going from big fixes (voice and structure) to small fixes (individual words and phrases).

lessons.title: The Iteration Loop
lessons.description: The back-and-forth that turns generic output into something you'd actually send.

sections.content: First output never ships. Three rounds: fix voice and structure, then specific sections, then individual words. Name what's wrong, protect what's working — never just say "make it better."
Start broad, then narrow, then polish. Two or three targeted rounds beats ten rounds of "make it better."

exercises.prompt: You're on Round 2 of editing a blog post with ChatGPT. The introduction is perfect but the conclusion feels rushed. What do you say?
options: The intro is great -- don't touch it. The conclusion feels rushed. Expand it with a specific call-to-action and a final thought that ties back to the opening story.
Add more words to the conclusion.
exercises.explanation: Option B does three things right: protects the good part ('don't touch the intro'), identifies the specific problem ('conclusion feels rushed'), and gives direction ('expand with CTA, tie back to opening'). The others are either too vague or too destructive.
hints: The best iteration feedback protects what works, names what's broken, and gives a specific direction.

exercises.prompt: ChatGPT wrote you a cold email that's generic and corporate. Write a revision prompt that fixes the voice and structure in one round.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"ChatGPT produced this cold email: 'Dear [Name], I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to introduce our consulting services, which help businesses optimize their operations and drive growth. With over 10 years of experience, we have helped numerous companies achieve their goals. I would love the opportunity to discuss how we can help your organization. Please let me know if you would be available for a brief call at your convenience. Best regards.' Write a single revision prompt that transforms this into something you'd actually send.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["drop","cut","remove","kill","no","don't","pleasantries","hope this finds"],"label":"Told ChatGPT what to remove","points":2},{"keyword_group":["open","lead","start","begin","first","hook","observation","specific"],"label":"Specified a new opening approach","points":1},{"keyword_group":["short","under","words","concise","brief","100"],"label":"Set a length constraint","points":1},{"keyword_group":["tone","sound","peer","casual","direct","not a salesperson","human","friend"],"label":"Defined the tone shift","points":1},{"keyword_group":["question","CTA","end with","close","next step","ask"],"label":"Specified the closing format","points":1},{"keyword_group":["their","business","company","about them","specific","research","personalize"],"label":"Asked for personalization","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Rewrite this. Drop the pleasantries entirely. Open with a specific observation about their business, not a greeting. Keep it under 100 words. Sound like a peer, not a salesperson. End with a question, not a request for a call. Make it something I'd actually want to read if it landed in my inbox.","tips":["Telling ChatGPT what to cut is often more effective than telling it what to add.","A length constraint forces ChatGPT to prioritize substance over filler.","'Sound like a peer, not a salesperson' gives ChatGPT a clear persona to target."]}
exercises.explanation: The best revision prompts tell ChatGPT what to cut, how to restructure, what tone to hit, and how to end. One specific revision prompt beats five rounds of 'make it better.'
hints: Think about everything that makes this email feel like AI slop. Now tell ChatGPT to fix all of it at once.

exercises.prompt: Walk through three rounds of iterating on a cold email, from generic AI output to something you'd actually send.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "The Three-Round Iteration Loop", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You asked ChatGPT to write a cold email to a potential consulting client. It came back generic: 'Dear [Name], I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to introduce our consulting services...' Classic AI slop. Time to iterate."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Round 1: The email is generic and sounds like every other cold email. What's your first revision prompt?", "question": "Which revision prompt fixes the most in one round?", "options": ["Make it better", "Rewrite this. Drop the pleasantries. Open with a specific observation about their business. Keep it under 100 words.", "Can you make it sound more professional?", "Add some humor to make it stand out"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Option B fixes voice, structure, length, and tone in one move. 'Make it better' gives nothing. 'More professional' makes it worse. Humor without fixing fundamentals is lipstick on a pig."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT rewrote with a sharper opening about their recent product launch. But the second half still reads stiff and the CTA asks them to 'schedule a call at your earliest convenience.'", "question": "Round 2: The opening is great but the back half is still stiff. What do you say?", "options": ["Change the whole thing", "The opening is great, keep that. But the second paragraph is too formal. Write it like I'm texting a friend who happens to be a CEO.", "Make the CTA better", "It's fine, just send it"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Protecting what works ('keep the opening') while targeting what doesn't ('second paragraph too formal') is the core iteration skill. Telling ChatGPT to rewrite everything throws away your progress."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "Round 3: Almost there. The email is punchy and personal but one sentence says 'leveraging synergies.' Write your final polish prompt to kill the jargon.", "placeholder": "Write your Round 3 polish prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["kill", "remove", "cut", "replace", "drop", "leveraging", "synergies", "jargon"], "label": "Targeted the specific jargon", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["plain", "simple", "real", "human", "English", "specific", "what you actually do"], "label": "Asked for plain language replacement", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["keep", "don't change", "rest", "everything else", "almost"], "label": "Protected the rest of the email", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Almost there. Kill 'leveraging synergies' and replace it with something specific about what you actually do, in plain English. No consulting jargon. Don't change anything else.", "max_points": 5, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Three rounds, four minutes. Round 1 sets voice and structure. Round 2 targets weak spots while protecting good parts. Round 3 polishes individual words. Each round gets more precise.
hints: Round 1: fix the big things. Round 2: fix specific sections. Round 3: polish individual words.

lessons.title: Document Analysis
lessons.description: Upload documents and have a conversation about them instead of slogging through them yourself.

sections.content: Upload a contract, report, or proposal and ask targeted questions — not "summarize this." Try: "Find every clause that could cost me money." Specific questions get specific answers.
Upload documents and ask targeted extraction questions. Upload multiple files together for cross-document comparison — ChatGPT holds all of them in context simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: Your boss dropped 3 vendor proposals on your desk (42, 28, and 35 pages). You need a comparison by end of day. What's your first move with ChatGPT?
options: Read all three yourself first, then ask ChatGPT to help format your notes
Upload all three to ChatGPT at once and ask for a comparison table across pricing, terms, SLAs, timelines, and red flags
Summarize each proposal in separate ChatGPT conversations, then compare the summaries yourself
Ask ChatGPT to summarize just the executive summaries of each
exercises.explanation: Uploading all three together lets ChatGPT compare directly across documents. Separate conversations lose the cross-document comparison power. Reading them yourself defeats the purpose. Executive summaries miss the details that matter.
hints: Think about what makes ChatGPT's document analysis powerful: holding all the data in context simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: You have a vendor contract that's 30 pages long. Write a prompt that gets ChatGPT to find the clauses that actually matter to you, not just a generic summary.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You just received a 30-page vendor contract for a SaaS platform your company wants to adopt. You need to review it before signing. Write a prompt that gets ChatGPT to find the specific clauses that could hurt you, not just summarize the document.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["auto-renewal","renewal","termination","cancel","exit","lock-in","commitment"],"label":"Asked about renewal or exit terms","points":2},{"keyword_group":["liability","indemnification","damages","risk","penalty","cost","fee"],"label":"Asked about liability or cost risks","points":1},{"keyword_group":["IP","intellectual property","ownership","data","rights","who owns"],"label":"Asked about IP or data ownership","points":1},{"keyword_group":["flag","red flag","concern","unusual","deviate","non-standard","watch out"],"label":"Asked ChatGPT to flag problems","points":2},{"keyword_group":["deadline","notice","days","period","timeline","date"],"label":"Asked about deadlines or notice periods","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Review this 30-page vendor contract. I need you to find: 1) Any auto-renewal clauses and what notice period is required to cancel. 2) Liability caps or indemnification terms that heavily favor the vendor. 3) Who owns data and IP created during the engagement. 4) Any non-standard clauses that deviate from typical SaaS agreements. Flag anything that could cost me money or lock me in.","tips":["Specific extraction requests ('find auto-renewal clauses') beat generic ones ('summarize this contract').","Asking ChatGPT to flag non-standard terms leverages its knowledge of typical contract patterns.","Connecting the analysis to consequences ('could cost me money') helps ChatGPT prioritize what matters."]}
exercises.explanation: The best document prompts tell ChatGPT exactly what to look for and why it matters. 'Summarize this contract' gets you a summary. 'Find anything that could cost me money' gets you actionable intelligence.
hints: Think about what keeps you up at night after signing a contract. Ask ChatGPT to find those specific things.

exercises.prompt: Walk through analyzing a contract clause that looks standard but actually contains a hidden risk.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Reading Between the Lines", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You uploaded a vendor contract to ChatGPT. Most of it looks standard. But ChatGPT flagged a clause in Section 12 for your attention: 'The Service Provider shall deliver all milestones within the agreed timeline. In the event of delays caused by circumstances beyond reasonable control, the timeline may be extended at the Provider's discretion. Client agrees to pay all invoiced amounts within 15 days of receipt, regardless of milestone completion status.'"}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT says: 'I found two issues in this clause that could hurt you.'", "question": "What's the first problem with 'timeline may be extended at the Provider's discretion'?", "options": ["It's standard force majeure language -- nothing unusual", "It means the vendor can delay indefinitely with no recourse for you, since 'at their discretion' requires no mutual agreement", "The timeline extension is fine as long as they communicate it", "This only applies to extreme circumstances so it's not a concern"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "'At the Provider's discretion' is the key phrase. Standard force majeure requires mutual agreement or defined triggers. This lets the vendor decide unilaterally. Change to 'by mutual written agreement.'"}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT says: 'The second issue is even more concerning. Look at the payment terms.'", "question": "What's wrong with 'pay all invoiced amounts within 15 days, regardless of milestone completion status'?", "options": ["Net-15 is too short -- it should be net-30", "You're paying for work that hasn't been delivered yet, with no leverage to ensure quality or completion", "The payment terms are standard for this type of contract", "15 days is fine if you have the cash flow"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "'Regardless of milestone completion status' means you pay whether work is done or not. Standard practice ties payment to milestone acceptance. This removes your biggest leverage: withholding payment until delivery."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "ChatGPT found the problems. Now write a prompt asking ChatGPT to draft the specific contract language you'd propose instead of these two problematic clauses.", "placeholder": "Write your prompt for ChatGPT to draft replacement clause language...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["mutual", "agreement", "both parties", "written", "consent"], "label": "Asked for mutual agreement on extensions", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["milestone", "delivery", "completion", "acceptance", "tied to", "upon"], "label": "Tied payment to milestone completion", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["replace", "rewrite", "redline", "alternative", "propose", "counter"], "label": "Asked for replacement language", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Draft replacement language for these two clauses. 1) Timeline extensions should require mutual written agreement, not provider discretion. 2) Payment should be tied to milestone acceptance -- we pay within 15 days of approving each deliverable, not on invoice receipt regardless of completion. Keep the language formal and contract-ready.", "max_points": 5, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: ChatGPT catches contract risks that humans gloss over. The key phrases ('at the Provider's discretion,' 'regardless of milestone completion') look harmless but shift all risk to you. ChatGPT reads every word with equal attention.
hints: Pay attention to phrases like 'at their discretion' and 'regardless of' -- these shift risk entirely to one party.

lessons.title: Research and Thinking
lessons.description: Use ChatGPT as a thinking partner, not a search engine. Progressive research that builds compounding knowledge.

sections.content: Don't ask "what's the market size?" Ask "I'm entering legal tech — what are the 3 biggest risks I'm not seeing?" Give context, ask for challenges, push back. That's a thinking partner.
Start broad, drill into specifics, apply to your context, then argue with the synthesis. The best insights come from the back-and-forth.

exercises.prompt: You're evaluating a business opportunity. Which prompt turns ChatGPT into a thinking partner instead of a search engine?
options: What's the market size for legal tech?
Give me a list of legal tech companies.
I'm considering entering legal tech with an AI intake product for personal injury firms. What are the three biggest risks I'm not seeing, and what assumptions am I making that might be wrong?
Is legal tech a good market?
exercises.explanation: The third prompt gives ChatGPT your specific context, asks for risks you haven't considered, and explicitly asks for assumption-checking. The others treat ChatGPT as a search engine that returns generic information.
hints: A thinking partner needs to know YOUR situation, not just the topic. Which prompt gives ChatGPT enough context to actually challenge your thinking?

exercises.prompt: Write the second prompt in a progressive research sequence about AI-powered customer service. The first prompt was 'What are the main approaches to AI customer service in mid-market SaaS?' and ChatGPT gave you an overview. Now drill deeper.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"ChatGPT just gave you an overview of AI customer service approaches: chatbots, conversational AI, ticket routing, knowledge base automation, and agent augmentation. Conversational AI caught your eye. Write the second prompt that drills deeper into that specific approach.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["conversational","AI","approach","second","that","expand","deeper","more"],"label":"Referenced ChatGPT's previous answer","points":2},{"keyword_group":["challenge","problem","fail","difficulty","hard","struggle","risk","limitation"],"label":"Asked about challenges or failure modes","points":2},{"keyword_group":["implement","build","deploy","real","practice","actually","work"],"label":"Asked about real-world implementation","points":1},{"keyword_group":["differ","separate","good","bad","effective","versus","compared","what makes"],"label":"Asked what separates good from bad","points":1},{"keyword_group":["cost","ROI","reduce","save","result","outcome","metric"],"label":"Asked about outcomes or metrics","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Expand on the conversational AI approach. What are the biggest implementation challenges mid-market SaaS companies face? What separates solutions that actually reduce support costs from ones that just add a chatbot nobody uses? Give me the real-world failure modes, not the marketing pitch.","tips":["Referencing ChatGPT's previous answer ('expand on the conversational AI approach') maintains the progressive thread.","Asking about failure modes gets you information that marketing materials hide.","'What separates good from bad' forces ChatGPT to give you discriminating criteria, not just a description."]}
exercises.explanation: The second prompt in a progressive sequence should reference ChatGPT's previous answer, drill into the most interesting area, and ask for the nuances that a surface-level overview misses.
hints: You already have the overview. Now ask for the nuances, challenges, and failure modes that separate theory from practice.

exercises.prompt: Walk through a thinking-partner conversation where ChatGPT challenges a founder's expansion plan and surfaces a blind spot.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "The Thinking Partner in Action", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "A founder asks ChatGPT: 'We're thinking about expanding from 1 city to 5 cities next quarter. Our product-market fit is strong. Good idea?' ChatGPT responds by questioning the assumption: 'PMF is strong -- but in how many customer segments? If it's strong in one niche within one city, that's different from PMF that transfers geographically.'"}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT identified three risks: 1) Your city might have weak incumbents, the next 5 might not. 2) Going 1-to-5 is 5x operational complexity, not 5x revenue. 3) Five simultaneous launches means five simultaneous cash burns.", "question": "ChatGPT suggests expanding to 2 cities first instead of 5. How should you respond?", "options": ["Ignore the advice -- we need to move fast before competitors catch up", "Ask ChatGPT to explain its reasoning: 'Why 2 instead of 5? What specifically would we learn from 2 that we can't learn from 5?'", "Agree immediately and scrap the 5-city plan", "Ask ChatGPT for a more optimistic analysis"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Engaging with the reasoning is the thinking-partner move. You're stress-testing the suggestion, not blindly accepting or rejecting. ChatGPT's reasoning might change your mind. Your pushback might change ChatGPT's."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT explains: 'Two cities test whether your playbook transfers. If both work, you have evidence for the remaining three. If one fails, you learn why at 1/5 the cost. Five simultaneous failures is a company-ending event. Two gives you a control group.'", "question": "You realize ChatGPT has a point about risk, but you're worried about timing. How do you push back productively?", "options": ["Just agree with ChatGPT and move on", "Tell ChatGPT: 'I hear you on risk. But our investor expects aggressive growth. How do we satisfy both -- de-risk AND show momentum?'", "Abandon the expansion plan entirely", "Ask ChatGPT to write a plan for 5 cities anyway"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "This is productive pushback. You accepted the risk analysis but introduced a constraint ChatGPT didn't know about (investor expectations). Now ChatGPT can synthesize both factors into a better plan."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "ChatGPT revised its recommendation: launch 2 cities in month 1, evaluate for 60 days, then launch the remaining 3 if metrics hit targets. Now write a prompt asking ChatGPT to define what 'metrics hit targets' actually means -- what specific numbers would prove the playbook transfers.", "placeholder": "Write your prompt defining success metrics...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["metric", "number", "target", "KPI", "benchmark", "measure", "threshold"], "label": "Asked for specific measurable targets", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["playbook", "transfer", "replicate", "work", "prove", "validate"], "label": "Connected metrics to playbook validation", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["timeline", "60 days", "when", "by when", "deadline", "evaluate"], "label": "Referenced the evaluation timeline", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Define the specific metrics that would prove our playbook transfers. What numbers do we need to hit in the 2 test cities within 60 days to confidently green-light the remaining 3? Give me the actual thresholds, not vague goals.", "max_points": 5, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: A productive thinking-partner conversation involves ChatGPT challenging assumptions, you pushing back with new constraints, and both sides iterating toward a better answer. The best decisions come from this back-and-forth.
hints: The goal isn't to agree or disagree with ChatGPT. It's to refine the decision through back-and-forth.

lessons.title: Power Prompts: Pre-Mortems, Steelmanning & Red-Teaming
lessons.description: Three named prompt engineering frameworks that force rigor into decisions: pre-mortems, steelmanning, and red-teaming. The prompts that make ChatGPT your best adversarial thinking partner.

sections.content: Pre-mortem: Assume it failed — what went wrong? Steelman: Make the strongest case for the other option. Red-team: Attack this plan. Be brutal.
Pre-mortem finds failure modes. Steelmanning surfaces the best opposing argument. Red-teaming attacks your specific execution. Run all three before high-stakes decisions.

exercises.prompt: You're choosing between three vendors and can't decide. Which ChatGPT prompt gives you the most useful analysis?
options: List the pros and cons of each vendor.

I'm choosing between three vendors. My criteria: cost (30%), integration ease (25%), support quality (25%), scalability (20%). Here's what I know about each. Build a weighted decision matrix and explain your scoring.
Tell me the best vendor for a startup.
exercises.explanation: A weighted decision matrix with explicit criteria gives you a structured, defensible analysis. 'Which should I pick?' is too vague. Pros/cons lists are unweighted. 'Best for a startup' ignores your specific situation.
hints: The most useful analysis gives you a framework you can adjust, not just an opinion.

exercises.prompt: You're about to launch a product. Write a prompt that uses a pre-mortem framework to surface risks you haven't considered.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're launching a B2B SaaS product next month. You have 3 beta customers, a Product Hunt launch planned, and $5K budget. Write a prompt that gets ChatGPT to run a pre-mortem -- imagining the launch already failed -- to surface risks you're not seeing.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["pre-mortem","failed","imagine","assume","went wrong","didn't work","flopped"],"label":"Used pre-mortem framing","points":2},{"keyword_group":["launch","product","SaaS","B2B","Product Hunt"],"label":"Provided launch context","points":1},{"keyword_group":["3","beta","customer","user","traction"],"label":"Mentioned current traction","points":1},{"keyword_group":["risk","blind spot","miss","not seeing","assumption","overlooked"],"label":"Asked for hidden risks","points":2},{"keyword_group":["$5K","budget","resource","constraint","limited"],"label":"Included resource constraints","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Run a pre-mortem on our product launch. Imagine it's 3 months from now and the launch failed. We had 3 beta customers, a Product Hunt launch, and $5K budget. What went wrong? Give me the 5 most likely failure modes, especially the ones I'm probably not thinking about. Don't sugarcoat it.","tips":["The pre-mortem framing ('imagine it failed') unlocks more honest risk analysis than asking 'what could go wrong.'","Asking for failure modes you're 'not thinking about' pushes ChatGPT past the obvious risks.","'Don't sugarcoat it' signals that you want honest assessment, not reassurance."]}
exercises.explanation: A pre-mortem prompt imagines the future failure and works backward to find causes. This framing surfaces risks that forward-looking analysis misses because it bypasses optimism bias.
hints: The pre-mortem trick is asking 'what went wrong?' instead of 'what could go wrong?' The past-tense framing produces more specific, honest answers.

exercises.prompt: Walk through using a pre-mortem and steelman framework to stress-test a product launch decision.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Stress-Testing Your Launch", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You're launching a B2B SaaS product next month. You have 3 beta customers, a Product Hunt launch planned, and $5K budget. You asked ChatGPT to run a pre-mortem. ChatGPT came back with: 'Failure mode #1: You got traffic from Product Hunt but zero conversions because your landing page speaks to developers, not the buyers who actually sign checks.'"}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT's pre-mortem hit a nerve. Your 3 beta customers were all developers who found you through technical channels. You haven't validated that decision-makers will pay.", "question": "What's the most productive way to explore this risk further?", "options": ["Dismiss it -- developers are our users and they'll convince their managers to buy", "Ask ChatGPT: 'If that's the risk, what's the fastest way to validate that decision-makers will pay before we spend $5K on a launch?'", "Scrap the Product Hunt launch entirely", "Ask ChatGPT for a more optimistic analysis"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Engaging with the risk and asking for a validation path is productive. Dismissing it leaves the blind spot. Scrapping the launch is overreaction. Asking for optimism defeats the pre-mortem."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT suggests a steelman: 'Before you commit to launching, let me make the strongest case for delaying 60 days. 1) You haven't validated pricing with anyone outside your network. 2) Your landing page targets developers but your buyer is a VP. 3) Product Hunt gives you one shot -- launching with unvalidated messaging wastes it.'", "question": "How should you respond to the steelman case for delay?", "options": ["Refuse to hear it -- we need to launch now", "Listen to all three points, then decide with eyes open whether the timing risk outweighs the delay risk", "Agree immediately and delay 60 days", "Ask ChatGPT to steelman the case for launching now too, so you can weigh both sides"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Hearing the steelman doesn't mean you'll delay. It means if you launch, that decision is informed rather than reflexive. You might still launch -- but you'll address the messaging gap first."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "You've decided to launch but address the messaging gap first. Write a prompt asking ChatGPT to red-team your landing page copy -- attacking every claim and assumption to find weaknesses before you spend $5K driving traffic to it.", "placeholder": "Write your red-team prompt for the landing page...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["red team", "attack", "critique", "tear apart", "brutal", "weakness", "flaw"], "label": "Used red-team framing", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["landing page", "copy", "messaging", "headline", "value prop"], "label": "Targeted the landing page specifically", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["buyer", "VP", "decision maker", "who pays", "non-technical", "manager"], "label": "Referenced the buyer persona gap", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["claim", "assumption", "promise", "believe", "trust"], "label": "Asked about claims or assumptions", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Red-team our landing page. Attack every claim, every assumption, every piece of copy. I need you to be brutal. Specifically: does this page convince a VP or department head to buy, or does it only make sense to a developer? What claims are we making that we can't back up? Where would a skeptical buyer lose interest?", "max_points": 5, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Pre-mortem surfaces risks. Steelman presents the strongest opposing case. Red-teaming attacks your specific execution. Used together, they turn a gut-feel launch into a stress-tested decision.
hints: Each framework serves a different purpose: pre-mortem finds risks, steelman presents alternatives, red-team attacks execution.
